stem: A 6-month-old, previously well male is brought to a metropolitan emergency department in late winter with 3 days of coryza and cough, now feeding about 60% of his usual volumes. He is alert and consolable in his mother's arms. On examination he has nasal flaring, tracheal tug, and mild subcostal recession. Bilateral fine inspiratory crackles and end-expiratory wheeze are audible throughout both lung fields. He has not received any prior treatment.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse rate 158/min, blood pressure 88/52 mmHg, respiratory rate 56/min, SpO2 88% on room air, temperature 37.8°C
data_table.Examination: Alert, consolable; nasal flaring, tracheal tug, mild subcostal recession; bilateral crackles and wheeze; perfusion warm
data_table.Investigations: CXR not performed; viral PCR pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial respiratory support?

options.text: Heated humidified high-flow nasal cannula at 2 L/kg/min as the initial form of respiratory support.
options.rationale: HFNC is not first-line in Australian practice for bronchiolitis: low-flow should be tried first and HFNC reserved for low-flow failure. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Nasal prong oxygen at 1 to 2 L/min titrated to maintain SpO2 at or above 90 percent.
options.rationale: Low-flow nasal prong oxygen is first-line for hypoxic bronchiolitis with SpO2 below 90%, with HFNC reserved for failure after 2 hours of low-flow. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Continuous positive airway pressure via a tight-fitting mask set initially at 7 cmH2O of pressure.
options.rationale: CPAP is escalation after HFNC failure or for impending respiratory failure, not first-line support in a consolable infant. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Nebulised salbutamol 2.5 mg via face mask repeated every 20 minutes for three doses.
options.rationale: Bronchodilators are not recommended for bronchiolitis in infants under 12 months and do not change the disease course. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Nebulised hypertonic 3 percent saline given with nebulised adrenaline 5 mL of 1 in 1000.
options.rationale: Routine nebulised hypertonic saline and adrenaline are not recommended for typical bronchiolitis outside research protocols in Australia. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

explanation.summary: Bronchiolitis in infants under 12 months is supportive: minimal handling, careful feeding, and oxygen only when SpO2 falls below 90% on room air. The Royal Children's Hospital Melbourne CPG specifies low-flow nasal prong oxygen at 1 to 2 L/min as the initial mode, with HFNC reserved for failure of low-flow after 2 hours. The 90% threshold is deliberately lower than the 92 to 94% used in some international guidelines, reflecting Australian evidence that higher targets prolong admission without benefit.
key_points: RCH SpO2 target in bronchiolitis is 90%, not 92 to 94%.
Low-flow nasal prongs at 1 to 2 L/min are first-line; HFNC is second-line.
Salbutamol, adrenaline, hypertonic saline, and steroids are not recommended in typical bronchiolitis under 12 months.
explanation.pearls: If SpO2 sits at 89 to 90% on room air in an otherwise stable, feeding infant, observe closely without oxygen first; oxygen only when SpO2 drops below 90% and stays there.

stem: A 2-year-old girl, weighed in clinic last week at 14 kg, has been brought into the emergency department 12 minutes after biting into a cashew biscuit at a family gathering. She is now floppy in her father's arms. Spreading urticaria covers her face and trunk and her lips are swollen. Brassy inspiratory stridor and audible wheeze are heard at the bedside. She has no prior cardiorespiratory disease and no documented allergy history. Intravenous access has been attempted once unsuccessfully.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse rate 175/min, blood pressure 75/45 mmHg, respiratory rate 44/min, SpO2 92% on room air, temperature 36.7°C
data_table.Examination: Floppy but rousable; widespread urticaria, lip swelling, stridor, wheeze; capillary refill 4 seconds
data_table.Investigations: Bedside glucose 4.8 mmol/L; tryptase to send after stabilisation

lead_in: What is the most appropriate adrenaline dose and route to give first?

options.text: Adrenaline 300 micrograms intramuscularly into the deltoid muscle of the right upper arm.
options.rationale: 300 mcg is the ASCIA dose for children over 20 kg, and deltoid is not the recommended IM site for paediatric anaphylaxis. (Source: ASCIA anaphylaxis guidelines)

options.text: Adrenaline 140 micrograms calculated as 10 mcg/kg intramuscularly into the lateral thigh.
options.rationale: ASCIA uses banded weight-based dosing for IM adrenaline rather than mg/kg arithmetic to avoid calculation errors in a crisis. (Source: ASCIA anaphylaxis guidelines)

options.text: Adrenaline 150 micrograms intramuscularly into the anterolateral mid-thigh muscle.
options.rationale: ASCIA bands by weight: 10 to 20 kg receives 150 mcg IM into the anterolateral thigh as first dose. (Source: ASCIA anaphylaxis guidelines)

options.text: Adrenaline 14 micrograms (1 mcg/kg) intravenously after gaining rapid intraosseous access.
options.rationale: IV adrenaline is reserved for refractory anaphylaxis with cardiac monitoring and experienced staff; IM is the first dose. (Source: ASCIA anaphylaxis guidelines)

options.text: Adrenaline 5 mL of 1 in 1000 via nebuliser to manage the stridor while obtaining IV access.
options.rationale: Nebulised adrenaline can help upper airway oedema as an adjunct but does not replace IM adrenaline as first-line treatment. (Source: ASCIA anaphylaxis guidelines)

explanation.summary: ASCIA bands IM adrenaline dosing by approximate weight to remove arithmetic errors during an acute event. A child of 10 to 20 kg gets 150 mcg IM (the EpiPen Jr equivalent dose), 20 kg and above gets 300 mcg, and 50 kg and above gets 500 mcg. The IM site is the anterolateral mid-thigh, which delivers reliable absorption even in shock. IV adrenaline is reserved for refractory cases under monitoring.
key_points: Anterolateral mid-thigh is the only recommended IM site in paediatric anaphylaxis.
Repeat IM adrenaline every 5 minutes until clinical response or commencement of adrenaline infusion.
explanation.pearls: If you cannot recall the band, an EpiPen Jr 150 mcg auto-injector is the right device for any child clearly in the 10 to 20 kg range; deploy it and call for help.

stem: A 4-day-old term male (born at 40+2 weeks, birth weight 3.4 kg) is brought to the general practice for review because his mother thinks he is more yellow than yesterday. He is exclusively breastfed, latching well, with about 6 heavy wet nappies per day and yellow seedy stool. He is alert and not floppy. Jaundice is visible to his chest but not below the umbilicus. Maternal blood group is O Rh-positive; baby cord blood direct antiglobulin test was negative. He has no risk factors for severe hyperbilirubinaemia. Total serum bilirubin at 96 hours is 305 micromol/L with a direct fraction of 8 micromol/L.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse rate 138/min, respiratory rate 42/min, SpO2 98% on room air, temperature 36.9°C, weight 3.32 kg (2.4 percent below birth weight)
data_table.Examination: Alert; jaundice extends to chest but not below umbilicus; no hepatosplenomegaly; tone normal
data_table.Investigations: TSB 305 micromol/L; direct 8 micromol/L; phototherapy threshold for this risk profile at 96 hours is 350 micromol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence intensive phototherapy in hospital and recheck the bilirubin level after 6 hours.
options.rationale: The TSB is 45 micromol/L below threshold and phototherapy is not yet indicated for this risk band. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal jaundice)

options.text: Stop breastfeeding for 24 hours and switch to formula to reduce enterohepatic recirculation.
options.rationale: Routinely stopping breastfeeding is not recommended and there is no evidence the jaundice is breastfeeding failure jaundice here. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal jaundice)

options.text: Give intravenous immunoglobulin while preparing the team for a double-volume exchange transfusion.
options.rationale: IVIG and exchange transfusion are for proven immune haemolysis or TSB nearing exchange threshold, neither of which apply here. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal jaundice)

options.text: Continue established breastfeeding, reassure the mother, and repeat the bilirubin in 24 hours.
options.rationale: TSB sits below the phototherapy threshold for a term well infant with no risk factors, so safety-netting with a repeat measurement is appropriate. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal jaundice)

options.text: Send urgent G6PD assay and commence empirical IV ampicillin and gentamicin pending blood cultures.
options.rationale: There are no features of sepsis or haemolysis to justify empirical antibiotics and additional testing at this point. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal jaundice)

explanation.summary: A term well infant on day 4 with TSB below the phototherapy threshold on the nomogram, a negative direct antiglobulin test, and adequate weight pattern needs ongoing breastfeeding support and a follow-up bilirubin rather than escalation. Stopping breastfeeding, starting phototherapy, or instituting sepsis treatment are reflexes to avoid when the numbers fit a benign trajectory. Document the threshold used so any repeat is interpreted on the same risk-banded curve.
key_points: Use the risk-banded nomogram to decide phototherapy, not an absolute TSB number.
Term well infants with negative DAT and no risk factors tolerate higher TSB before phototherapy.
Do not stop breastfeeding routinely; address jaundice without disrupting feeding wherever possible.
explanation.pearls: If TSB is within 50 micromol/L of the phototherapy threshold, repeat in 6 to 12 hours rather than 24, because the trajectory matters as much as the absolute number.

stem: A 3-year-old boy weighing 14 kg presents to a regional emergency department with 14 hours of fever and an evolving non-blanching purpuric rash on his trunk and lower limbs. He is rousable but increasingly irritable. He looks mottled with cool peripheries. Two attempts at peripheral intravenous access by experienced staff have failed; an intraosseous kit is open and on the trolley. The on-call paediatric retrieval team has been notified.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse rate 175/min, blood pressure 78/40 mmHg, respiratory rate 42/min, SpO2 94% on room air, temperature 39.8°C
data_table.Examination: Rousable but irritable, capillary refill 5 seconds, mottled skin, spreading purpura on trunk; no neck stiffness
data_table.Investigations: VBG: pH 7.22, lactate 5.6 mmol/L; bedside glucose 3.4 mmol/L; blood culture bottles primed

lead_in: What is the correct order of the next four interventions over the next 15 minutes?

options.text: Intraosseous access, 20 mL/kg saline bolus, blood cultures, then IV ceftriaxone afterwards.
options.rationale: Giving fluid before cultures and antibiotics delays time-critical antimicrobial therapy in meningococcal shock. (Source: RCH CPG: Sepsis)

options.text: Intraosseous access, IV ceftriaxone, 20 mL/kg saline bolus, then adrenaline infusion 0.1 mcg/kg/min.
options.rationale: Inotropes should follow at least 40 to 60 mL/kg of fluid resuscitation, not the first 20 mL/kg. (Source: RCH CPG: Sepsis)

options.text: Blood cultures peripherally, then IV ceftriaxone, then 20 mL/kg saline, intraosseous as backup.
options.rationale: Persisting with peripheral access in shocked children wastes minutes; intraosseous access is the next step after two failed attempts. (Source: RCH CPG: Sepsis)

options.text: Saline bolus 20 mL/kg via IO, blood cultures, IV ceftriaxone, then commence noradrenaline infusion.
options.rationale: Cultures should be taken before any antibiotic dose where possible, and noradrenaline is not the first vasoactive in paediatric septic shock. (Source: RCH CPG: Sepsis)

options.text: Intraosseous access, blood cultures drawn, IV ceftriaxone given, then 20 mL/kg saline bolus.
options.rationale: RCH sepsis pathway: secure IO/IV access, take cultures, give the first IV antibiotic within the hour, then a 20 mL/kg crystalloid bolus over 5 to 10 minutes. (Source: RCH CPG: Sepsis)

explanation.summary: In suspected meningococcal sepsis, every minute to first IV antibiotic matters. The RCH and ANZICS Paediatric Sepsis Pathway codifies the order as access then cultures then antibiotic then fluid, with the first antibiotic targeted within 60 minutes of recognition. Cultures must precede the antibiotic dose where they can be drawn at the time of access, because a single IO sample preserves diagnostic yield. Fluid resuscitation follows in 20 mL/kg aliquots reassessed for signs of overload before each repeat.
key_points: Sequence: access then cultures then antibiotic then fluid.
Intraosseous access after two failed peripheral attempts.
Fluid in 20 mL/kg aliquots up to 60 mL/kg, then escalate to inotropes.
explanation.pearls: If the IO sample volume is limited, prioritise blood culture bottles before VBG and other point-of-care tests, then push the antibiotic through the same IO.

stem:
A 9-year-old girl weighing 28 kg is in the paediatric high-dependency unit at hour 6 of a new-presentation diabetic ketoacidosis pathway. She received a 10 mL/kg saline bolus on arrival and is now on maintenance 0.9% saline with potassium and an insulin infusion at 0.05 units/kg/hr. Initial pH was 6.92 with glucose 38 mmol/L and ketones 5.8 mmol/L; current glucose is 19 mmol/L. Over the last 30 minutes her mother says she became hard to rouse and complained of a headache.

On re-examination she opens her eyes to voice, says inappropriate words, and localises to pain. Her left pupil is 5 mm and sluggish; the right is 3 mm and reactive. The bedside nurse has just rechecked her observations.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse rate 70/min (down from 120/min an hour ago), blood pressure 138/88 mmHg, respiratory rate 14/min, SpO2 97% on room air, temperature 36.6°C
data_table.Examination: GCS 11 (E3 V3 M5); left pupil 5 mm sluggish, right pupil 3 mm reactive; chest clear; perfusion adequate
data_table.Investigations: VBG pH 7.12; glucose 19 mmol/L; sodium 138, potassium 3.6, ketones 4.2 mmol/L
data_table.Medications: 0.9% saline 75 mL/hr with KCl 40 mmol/L; insulin infusion 0.05 units/kg/hr

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate intervention?

options.text: Give 3 percent hypertonic saline 5 mL/kg over 15 minutes and reduce maintenance fluid rate by one third.
options.rationale: Acute cerebral oedema in paediatric DKA is treated empirically with hypertonic saline or mannitol plus a fluid rate reduction, without waiting for imaging. (Source: RCH CPG: DKA)

options.text: Intubate and hyperventilate to PaCO2 25 mmHg until a CT brain has confirmed cerebral oedema.
options.rationale: Aggressive hyperventilation below PaCO2 30 mmHg worsens cerebral perfusion and outcomes in DKA-related cerebral oedema. (Source: RCH CPG: DKA)

options.text: Give mannitol 1 g/kg intravenously over 10 minutes and continue current fluid and insulin rates.
options.rationale: Mannitol is an acceptable alternative osmotherapy, but the maintenance fluid rate must also be reduced by about one third when treating cerebral oedema. (Source: RCH CPG: DKA)

options.text: Stop the insulin infusion, give 2 mL/kg of 10 percent dextrose intravenously, and recheck the glucose level.
options.rationale: Glucose is 19 mmol/L, not hypoglycaemic, and the deterioration is neurological rather than glycaemic. (Source: RCH CPG: DKA)

options.text: Give a 20 mL/kg 0.9 percent saline bolus while arranging an urgent CT brain to exclude haemorrhage.
options.rationale: Additional fluid boluses worsen cerebral oedema, and imaging must not delay osmotherapy when classical signs are present. (Source: RCH CPG: DKA)

explanation.summary: Cerebral oedema typically presents 4 to 12 hours into DKA treatment with headache, declining conscious state, bradycardia with hypertension (Cushing response), and pupil changes. Australian and ISPAD practice favours 3% hypertonic saline 2.5 to 5 mL/kg over 15 minutes as the preferred hyperosmolar therapy, paired with a reduction in maintenance fluid rate by about one third. Treatment is empirical at the bedside; CT confirms the diagnosis but must not delay therapy. Hyperventilation below PaCO2 30 mmHg is associated with worse outcomes.
key_points: Bradycardia with hypertension and altered consciousness in DKA equals cerebral oedema until proven otherwise.
3 percent saline 2.5 to 5 mL/kg over 15 minutes is preferred AU osmotherapy.
Reduce maintenance fluid rate by approximately one third when treating cerebral oedema.
explanation.pearls: Treat the moment the constellation appears: every additional fluid bolus or hour of normal-rate maintenance worsens midline shift before imaging confirms it.
